players.name: David Ospina
players.bio: Colombia's most-capped keeper with 130 appearances, Ospina is the undisputed guardian of Los Cafeteros. A veteran of Arsenal and Napoli, he returned home to Atlético Nacional for this World Cup swansong at 37.
players.facts: Ospina has more Colombia caps than any other goalkeeper in history — by a country mile.

players.name: Daniel Muñoz
players.bio: The Amalfi-born right-back brings Premier League steel to Colombia's flank. Dynamic and hard to shake off, Muñoz has made Crystal Palace's right side his own and arrives at his first World Cup full of confidence.
players.facts: Muñoz hails from Amalfi, Colombia — no connection to the Italian coast, but equally hard to get past.

players.name: Jhon Lucumí
players.bio: Lucumí is the composed, ball-playing centre-back who helped Bologna reach the Champions League. Cool under pressure and strong in the air, he's a key pillar of Colombia's defensive rebuild at just 27.
players.facts: Lucumí was part of the Bologna side that ended a 60-year wait to return to European football in 2024.

players.name: Santiago Arias
players.bio: A seasoned right-back with 68 caps, Arias is the experienced backup option who knows the international game inside out. Now back in South America with Independiente, he brings veteran calm to the squad.
players.facts: Arias spent time at Atlético Madrid and PSV — not bad for a lad from Medellín.

players.name: Kevin Castaño
players.bio: Born in 2000, Castañero is one of Colombia's bright young engines in midfield. Disciplined and combative at River Plate, the 25-year-old arrives at his debut World Cup ready to prove he belongs at this level.
players.facts: Castañero was born on 29 September 2000 — a Y2K baby now playing at the biggest tournament on earth.

players.name: Richard Ríos
players.bio: From the tiny town of Vegachí to Benfica's Estádio da Luz — Ríos has had quite the journey. A tenacious defensive midfielder with a surprising eye for goal, he's become indispensable to Colombia's engine room.
players.facts: Ríos grew up in one of Colombia's smallest municipalities — Vegachí has a population of just a few thousand.

players.name: Luis Díaz
players.bio: One of the most electrifying wingers on the planet, Díaz terrorises defenders with blistering pace and flair. Now lighting up Bayern Munich, the Guajira-born forward is Colombia's most dangerous weapon at 29.
players.facts: Luis Díaz's father was briefly kidnapped in 2023 while Luis was playing for Liverpool — he scored that very week.

players.name: Jorge Carrascal
players.bio: A creative force from Cartagena, Carrascal has flourished at Flamengo after stints in Europe. Skilful and unpredictable in the final third, he brings Brazilian flair-by-adoption to Colombia's attack.
players.facts: Carrascal won the Copa Libertadores with River Plate in 2018 before finding his best form in Brazil.

players.name: Jhon Córdoba
players.bio: A powerful target man with 6 international goals, Córdoba offers Colombia an aerial threat and physical presence up front. His career has taken him from the Bundesliga to Russia's Krasnodar, always finding the net.
players.facts: At 6ft 4in, Córdoba is one of the tallest strikers at the tournament — headers are very much his department.

players.name: James Rodríguez
players.bio: The magician in the No.10 shirt. James's 2014 World Cup Golden Boot remains the stuff of legend. Now with Minnesota United, he's written a remarkable second act — and Colombia fans believe there's one more chapter left.
players.facts: James's volley against Uruguay at Brazil 2014 was voted the best goal in World Cup history by FIFA fans.

players.name: Jhon Arias
players.bio: The Quibdó-born winger has been a revelation at Palmeiras, combining pace, directness and a knack for the big moment. Arias is Colombia's right-sided spark who can turn a game on its head in an instant.
players.facts: Jhon Arias shares a surname with teammate Santiago Arias — no relation, just a very Colombian coincidence.

players.name: Camilo Vargas
players.bio: Bogotá's own shot-stopper, Vargas has been a reliable presence in Colombia's squad for years. At 37, the Atlas keeper brings veteran composure as third-choice guardian behind the sticks at his possible final tournament.
players.facts: Vargas has spent much of his career in Mexico's Liga MX — making him well-acquainted with one of the host nations.

players.name: Yerry Mina
players.bio: Football's most joyful dancer doubles as a commanding centre-back. Mina's iconic goal celebrations and his headers haunt opponents in equal measure. His 8 international goals are extraordinary for a defender.
players.facts: Mina scored in the 93rd minute to send Colombia through at the 2018 World Cup — then danced. Obviously.

players.name: Gustavo Puerta
players.bio: At just 22, Puerta is one of the tournament's most exciting young midfielders. Already scoring on the international stage, the Racing Santander man is Colombia's glimpse into a very bright future.
players.facts: Puerta was born in 2003 — meaning he was just 11 when James Rodríguez won the Golden Boot in Brazil.

players.name: Juan Portilla
players.bio: The Cali-born central midfielder has built his career in South America, bringing energy and intelligence to Colombia's midfield. With 10 caps and growing experience at Athletico Paranaense, Portilla is on the rise.
players.facts: Portilla plays his club football in Brazil, giving him a unique tactical education ahead of this World Cup.

players.name: Jefferson Lerma
players.bio: The warrior of Colombia's midfield. Lerma's relentless pressing and no-nonsense style have made him a Premier League favourite at Crystal Palace. He's the engine that lets the flair players ahead of him shine.
players.facts: Lerma spent six seasons at Bournemouth, becoming a fan favourite — even during their yo-yo Championship years.

players.name: Johan Mojica
players.bio: An attack-minded left-back who loves to get forward, Mojica has been a reliable presence in Colombia's squad for years. Plying his trade at Mallorca in La Liga, he brings Spanish football savvy to the tournament.
players.facts: Mojica has played in Spain, Italy, France and Germany — one of Colombian football's most well-travelled full-backs.

players.name: Willer Ditta
players.bio: A rugged centre-back from Ibirico with just 5 caps, Ditta is the squad's defensive wildcard. Now at Cruz Azul in Mexico, he arrives on home-host soil and will relish any opportunity to make his mark.
players.facts: Ditta plays in Mexico — one of the three host nations — giving him a genuine home advantage feel in 2026.

players.name: Cucho Hernández
players.bio: Few players at this tournament are as breathtakingly athletic as Cucho. The acrobatic striker from Pereira is famous for gravity-defying goals and has now earned his shot at a major tournament with Real Betis.
players.facts: Cucho scored one of MLS's most outrageous bicycle kicks for Columbus Crew — the internet never recovered.

players.name: Juan Fernando Quintero
players.bio: The little maestro from Medellín, Quintero sees passes others can't imagine. A two-time Copa Libertadores winner with River Plate, JuanFer's left foot is considered a national treasure in Colombia.
players.facts: Quintero scored a stunning free-kick in River Plate's 2018 Copa Libertadores final at the Bernabéu.

players.name: Jaminton Campaz
players.bio: An exciting left winger with rapid feet and direct running, Campaz has built his reputation in Argentine football with Rosario Central. His 10 caps suggest a player still hungry for more — much more.
players.facts: Campaz is from Ibagué, known as Colombia's 'Music Capital' — and he plays with a similar sense of rhythm.

players.name: Deiver Machado
players.bio: A dependable left-back from Tadó, Machado has carved out a solid career in France with Nantes. Calm in possession and solid defensively, he's the unsung cover option Colombia can always rely on.
players.facts: Tadó, where Machado was born, is a small municipality in Chocó — one of Colombia's most biodiverse regions.

players.name: Davinson Sánchez
players.bio: A colossus at the back, Sánchez made his name at Ajax and Spurs before finding a new home at Galatasaray. With 79 caps and 4 goals, the Caloto-born defender is a tournament-hardened leader at the heart of defence.
players.facts: Sánchez joined Ajax from Atlético Nacional in 2017 and became one of Europe's most sought-after defenders almost overnight.

players.name: Álvaro Montero
players.bio: The third-choice keeper with a starring role in Argentine football, Montero has been a consistent performer for Vélez Sarsfield. His 12 caps show a reliable option should Colombia need him between the sticks.
players.facts: Montero hails from El Molino in La Guajira — the same region that produced Luis Díaz.

players.name: Luis Suárez
players.bio: Not that Luis Suárez. Colombia's version — born in Uruguay but raised Colombian — is a clinical finisher with 5 goals in just 12 caps at Sporting CP. He's answered every question asked of him at international level.
players.facts: Yes, Colombia's Luis Suárez was actually born in Uruguay. The confusion at roll call must be spectacular.

players.name: Andrés Gómez
players.bio: A young forward making waves at Vasco da Gama in Brazil, Gómez has already bagged 2 goals in 8 caps. At just 23, this World Cup could be the launchpad for a career that's barely getting started.
players.facts: Gómez scored 2 international goals before the age of 23 — a ratio that has Colombia very excited about his future.
